bibliographic_data.title: Towards Environmentally Equitable AI via Geographical Load Balancing
bibliographic_data.abstract: Fueled by the soaring popularity of foundation models, the accelerated growth of artificial intelligence (AI) models’ enormous environmental footprint has come under increased scrutiny. While many approaches have been proposed to make AI more energy-efficient and environmentally friendly, environmental inequity — the fact that AI’s environmental footprint can be disproportionately higher in certain regions than in others — has emerged, raising social-ecological justice concerns. This paper takes a first step toward addressing AI’s environmental inequity by fairly balancing its regional environmental impact. Concretely, we focus on the carbon and water footprints of AI model inference and propose equity-aware geographical load balancing (eGLB) to explicitly minimize AI’s highest environmental cost across all the regions.

core_content.novelty_claim: The paper proposes equity-aware geographical load balancing (eGLB) to minimize AI’s highest environmental cost across all regions, addressing the issue of environmental inequity.
core_content.key_methodology: The authors introduce auxiliary variables and optimize GLB decisions online based on dual mirror descent. They also run trace-based empirical simulations considering a set of geographically distributed data centers serving inference requests for a large language AI model.
outcomes: Existing load balancing approaches may amplify environmental inequity, while eGLB can significantly reduce regional disparities in terms of carbon and water footprints.
core_content.full_text_markdown: The paper addresses the growing environmental footprint of artificial intelligence (AI) models, particularly large language models like GPT-3. It highlights that AI's environmental impact is not uniformly distributed across regions, leading to environmental inequity. The authors propose a new approach called equity-aware geographical load balancing (eGLB) to minimize the highest environmental cost across all regions by focusing on carbon and water footprints during inference. They introduce auxiliary variables and use dual mirror descent for online optimization of GLB decisions. Empirical simulations demonstrate that eGLB can significantly reduce regional disparities in terms of carbon and water footprints, whereas existing approaches may exacerbate these disparities.
